source_intake_record.raw_question_text: The African bombardier beetle (\textit{Stenaptinus insignis}) can emit a jet of defensive spray from the movable tip of its abdomen. The beetle’s body has reservoirs containing two chemicals; when the beetle is disturbed, these chemicals combine in a reaction chamber, producing a compound that is warmed from 20\,^{\circ}\text{C} to 100\,^{\circ}\text{C} by the heat of reaction. The high pressure produced allows the compound to be sprayed out at speeds up to 19\,\text{m/s} (68\,\text{km/h}), scaring away predators of all kinds. (The beetle shown in \textbf{figure} is 2\,\text{cm} long.) Assume that the specific heat of the chemicals and of the spray is the same as that of water, 4.19 \times 10^3\,\text{J/kg·K}, and that the initial temperature of the chemicals is 20\,^{\circ}\text{C}. Calculate the heat of reaction of the two chemicals (in J/kg).
